findings.title: Rate limiting completely absent
findings.what: No rate limiting middleware exists on any endpoint — auth, read, or write
findings.why: An attacker can brute-force the /api/v1/auth/login endpoint indefinitely, enumerate user accounts, or launch a denial-of-service attack against any endpoint without throttling
findings.fix: Add slowapi middleware. Apply 5 req/min to /api/v1/auth/login, 60 req/min to write endpoints, 300 req/min to read endpoints. Store rate limit state in memory or Redis.

findings.title: Audit logs absent for user mutations and settings changes
findings.what: No audit log entries created when users are created/modified/deleted or when business settings change
findings.why: A compromised admin account could modify users or settings with no forensic trail. Cannot detect unauthorized privilege escalation or configuration tampering after the fact
findings.fix: Add audit_log entries in user_service (create, update, delete) and settings_service (update). Use existing AuditLog model. Log: actor_id, action, entity_type, entity_id, payload diff, timestamp.

findings.title: Default admin password 'Admin123!' hardcoded as fallback
findings.what: ADMIN_INITIAL_PASSWORD defaults to 'Admin123!' in config.py and is present in the .env file
findings.why: If the install script is skipped or .env not customized, the admin account has a known default password attackable via credential stuffing
findings.fix: Remove default value from config.py (make it required). Force password change on first login. Generate a random password in the install script and display it once only.

findings.title: JWT access token expiry set to 8 hours (480 minutes)
findings.what: ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES defaults to 480 — tokens remain valid for a full work shift even after logout
findings.why: A stolen token (via XSS, network sniff, or log leak) remains valid for up to 8 hours. No token revocation mechanism exists to invalidate a token before expiry.
findings.fix: Reduce to 60 minutes. Implement refresh-token rotation or a token revocation list (Redis set of invalidated JTIs). Log out should invalidate the token server-side.

findings.title: SSRF risk: logo_url downloaded server-side without domain allowlist
findings.what: Print Bridge fetches logo_url via urllib.request.urlopen with no domain validation, and backend passes any logo_url stored in business settings
findings.why: An admin-level attacker can set logo_url to an internal network address (e.g., http://192.168.1.1/admin) causing Print Bridge to probe internal services. Also enables request forgery against internal HTTP endpoints.
findings.fix: Validate logo_url is HTTPS with an allowlisted domain in business_settings validator. In Print Bridge, also validate URL scheme and hostname before fetching. Add 5-second timeout and 2MB max size (already has 5s timeout — add size limit).

findings.title: Missing CSP and HSTS headers in Caddy reverse proxy
findings.what: Caddyfile includes X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, X-XSS-Protection but is missing Content-Security-Policy and Strict-Transport-Security
findings.why: Absence of CSP leaves XSS attacks unmitigated at transport layer. Missing HSTS means if HTTPS is ever enabled, browsers will not enforce it, allowing SSL-strip attacks.
findings.fix: Add: header Content-Security-Policy "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; font-src 'self'". Add HSTS when HTTPS is configured.

findings.title: Tenant isolation not enforced at DB query level
findings.what: All service queries do not filter by tenant_id. Models have tenant_id with a hardcoded default UUID but queries never enforce tenant boundaries
findings.why: If multi-tenant deployment is ever attempted, all data from all tenants would be accessible to any authenticated user. Also allows future IDOR bugs if tenant_id filtering is added inconsistently.
findings.fix: Add tenant_id to all WHERE clauses now, even as single-tenant. Create a TenantContext dependency that injects tenant_id from the authenticated user. Fail closed: 404 if entity.tenant_id != current_user.tenant_id.

findings.title: SQLAlchemy echo enabled in non-production environments
findings.what: database.py uses echo=not settings.is_production, logging all SQL statements including WHERE clause values
findings.why: SQL logs can expose customer names, product names, and partial financial data. If development logs are forwarded to a log aggregator, PII leaks to that system.
findings.fix: Set echo=False by default. Add a separate DEBUG_SQL=false env var to enable selectively. Never enable echo in environments that process real customer data.

findings.title: backend/.env.test committed to version control
findings.what: backend/.env.test contains test database credentials and a fixed SECRET_KEY and is tracked by git
findings.why: Although credentials are for a test DB (not real secrets), this establishes a bad pattern. It also exposes the exact SECRET_KEY format expected. Developers may accidentally base production secrets on test values.
findings.fix: Add backend/.env.test to .gitignore. Use environment variables or a CI secret for test runs. Provide a .env.test.example with placeholder values instead.

findings.title: next.config.ts has no security headers configuration
findings.what: Frontend Next.js app defines no headers() function in next.config.ts — relies entirely on the Caddy proxy for security headers
findings.why: If frontend is accessed directly (dev server, behind a different proxy, or Caddy misconfiguration), browsers receive no security headers. Especially relevant during development where Caddy is not used.
findings.fix: Add headers() export to next.config.ts with X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and CSP. Defense-in-depth: headers at both proxy and app level.

findings.title: CORS allows all methods and headers (origins correctly restricted)
findings.what: CORSMiddleware uses allow_methods=['*'] and allow_headers=['*']. Origins are properly restricted to configured env var (default: localhost only)
findings.why: Low risk given origin restriction. However, wildcard methods/headers in combination with allow_credentials=True could be tightened. If origins are misconfigured to '*' in production, this becomes exploitable.
findings.fix: Consider restricting to explicit methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'] and headers: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization']. Verify CORS_ORIGINS env var is always set explicitly in production deployments.

findings.title: No multi-factor authentication for admin or supervisor accounts
findings.what: Authentication is username+password only. No TOTP, hardware key, or second factor exists for privileged roles.
findings.why: If admin credentials are compromised (phishing, reuse), an attacker gains full system access with no second barrier. Especially relevant since admin can void sales, modify settings, and view all reports.
findings.fix: Add TOTP (Google Authenticator/Authy) as an optional second factor, required for admin and supervisor roles. Libraries: pyotp + QR code enrollment flow.
